goal: Fix CommitNote overwrite bug: upsert (append intents) instead of NotesAdd -f overwrite, so two intents landing on the same main commit don't kick each other out

summary.title: CommitNote upsert: merge instead of overwrite when two intents land on one commit
summary.what: New engine/notes.go exposing upsertCommitNote(git, commit, addition): reads any existing mainline.commit_note on the commit, dedup-merges addition.Intents and addition.Reverts into it, and writes the result back. Note-level metadata (added_at, added_by, via, match_strategy) reflects the latest writer; per-intent fields (seal_result_hash) keep the existing entry to avoid silently rewriting older facts. Service.Merge, Service.Reconcile and Service.ReconcileManual now use upsertCommitNote instead of raw NotesAdd. Test suite: unit tests for mergeIntentRefs/mergeStringSets, integration tests for the two-intent-same-commit append, foreign-payload replacement and dedupe; rapid PBT TestPropertyUpsertNeverDropsIntents proving any sequence of single-intent upserts ends with the union as the final intent set; TestReconcileDoesNotEvictExistingIntent repros the exact dogfood failure (intent B sealed against intent A's merge commit, B's reconcile via tree_hash hits A's commit) and asserts both intents now coexist in the note.
summary.why: rc4 dogfooding (./mainline reconcile after fix/reconcile-tree-match merged) immediately exposed: NotesAdd uses 'git notes add -f' which overwrites the entire note. When tree_hash matched a second intent to the same commit that already carried a first intent's note, the second write silently dropped the first reference. The dropped intent then re-appeared as 'proposed' on the next sync (no note pointing to it), looking like a regression. Append-merge is the correct behaviour because CommitNote.Intents is already a list — the schema always intended one commit can carry multiple intents (squash of multiple changes), the writer just wasn't honouring it.
summary.user_goal: Stop reconcile / merge from silently kicking other intents off a shared commit when the same commit gets stamped twice.

decisions.point: Read-modify-write vs schema change for per-intent attribution
decisions.chose: Pure read-modify-write at the writer; keep CommitNote schema unchanged
decisions.rationale: The schema already supports multiple intents per note. Per-intent via/match_strategy attribution would be nicer for audit but is a separable schema change; this fix is small and unblocks dogfood today.
rejected: Add per-intent IntentReference.via / .match_strategy fields — bigger schema change, would need a migration for already-on-disk notes.

decisions.point: Conflict resolution when adding an intent that's already on the commit
decisions.chose: Existing entry wins; addition is silently dropped
decisions.rationale: A reconcile note for intent X arriving second carries a freshly-computed seal_result_hash from the current view. If the existing entry's hash differs that's a real audit concern, but blindly overwriting it would be worse — we'd lose the original sealing context. alreadyHasIntent in Reconcile already short-circuits this path, so the dedup branch fires only on programmer error, not normal flow.
rejected: Last-write-wins on per-intent fields — would silently rewrite seal_result_hash, defeating the audit purpose of the field.

decisions.point: Foreign (non-mainline.commit_note) payload found on the ref
decisions.rationale: refs/notes/mainline/intents is mainline-owned; finding a different kind there is itself a bug. We log it implicitly via the kind field flip and move on. Preserving foreign content would just compound a wrong state.

decisions.chose: PBT lives in notes_upsert_property_test.go with //go:build !quick; integration cases stay in notes_upsert_test.go
decisions.rationale: Keeps make quick-test < ~15s while ensuring the property is still part of normal go test runs.

risks: If the upstream alreadyHasIntent guard ever regresses, the dedup branch silently swallows the addition — would manifest as 'reconcile reports success but the seal_result_hash on the note was not refreshed'. The PBT covers absence of intents but not stale per-intent fields; consider a follow-up assertion.

Concurrent writers to the same notes ref still race: two processes can both read the same baseline, merge-and-write, and the second NotesAdd -f wins, silently dropping whichever intents only the first writer added. Same race exists pre-fix, just less visible. Real solution requires push-with-precondition (compare-and-swap on the notes ref tip).
followups: Per-intent via/match_strategy fields if audit needs to distinguish 'A came in via merge, B came in via reconcile_auto' on a multi-intent commit.
CAS-style concurrent upsert: read note tree, prepare new tree, push notes ref with --force-with-lease equivalent.
Audit warning when upsert finds a stale seal_result_hash for an existing IntentID (current code silently keeps the old one).

architectural_claims: All writes to refs/notes/mainline/intents from the engine go through upsertCommitNote.
CommitNote.Intents is canonically a set keyed by IntentID; the writer enforces this on every update.
behavioral_changes: Service.Merge / Reconcile / ReconcileManual now preserve any pre-existing intents on the same commit instead of overwriting them.
Two intents legitimately landing on the same main commit (multi-PR squash, post-merge reconcile race against an existing merge note, etc.) both end up listed in the single note for that commit.

api_changes.signature: engine.upsertCommitNote(git *gitops.Git, commit string, addition domain.CommitNote) error

security_implications: An attacker with write access to refs/notes/mainline/intents could still craft a conflicting CommitNote that includes their own forged IntentReference; upsert preserves their entry on the next legitimate write. Same trust model as before; no signing in v0.1.
migration_notes: Notes already written by older engine versions (single-intent or with via=merge/reconcile/manual) parse unchanged; first upsert against such a note merges the new intent in cleanly.
